Daulatabad Fort

# Daulatabad Fort

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Mar 07, 2021

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

1

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!rneK!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F613eca09-9592-4711-8a0f-0d8dca208569_550x366.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!rneK!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F613eca09-9592-4711-8a0f-0d8dca208569_550x366.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Daulatabad Fort is a marvel of medieval military architecture, and its security mechanisms are nothing short of ingenious. Built on a conical hill, the fort was designed to be nearly impregnable. Its creators combined natural topography with brilliant engineering to create a fortress that could withstand even the most determined invaders.

Originally known as Devagiri, this formidable hill fortress was founded by the Yadava king Bhillama V in 1187 CE. Perched atop a 200-meter conical hill near Aurangabad, Maharashtra, it was a symbol of strategic brilliance and architectural might.

It was captured by Allaudin Khilji and Malik Kafur in 1308, integrating it into the Delhi Sultanate. In 1327, Muhammad bin Tughlaq made a bold — and ultimately disastrous — decision: he shifted the entire capital of his empire from Delhi to Devagiri, renaming it Daulatabad, meaning *“Abode of Wealth.”*

He ordered the mass migration of Delhi’s population, believing Daulatabad’s central location would better serve his empire. But the move backfired due to logistical nightmares and water scarcity.By 1334, the capital was shifted back to Delhi, leaving behind a trail of suffering and chaos. This misadventure earned Tughlaq the infamous title of *“Mad King.”*

Till 1526 it was under the Bahmani Sultans, who fortified it further. It later oscillated between the Nizam Shahi dynasty of Ahmednagar and the Mughals, with Aurangzeb using it as a base for his Deccan campaigns. After Aurangzeb’s death, the fort became part of the Nizam of Hyderabad’s dominion, marking its final chapter in medieval power politics.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!-sCr!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe92d1179-cb35-4b27-87f7-b60f62aa8f42_602x339.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!-sCr!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe92d1179-cb35-4b27-87f7-b60f62aa8f42_602x339.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Standing at 210 feet (64 meters), the Chand Minar is one of the tallest minarets in India and a prominent landmark within Daulatabad Fort. It was built in 1445 CE by Ala-ud-din Bahmani to commemorate his conquest of the fort. Inspired by the Qutb Minar in Delhi, it’s a stunning example of Indo-Islamic architecture, adorned with Persian blue tiles.

The 4 Storeyed tower with 24 chambers has a small mosque at its base, with balconies supported by ornate brackets, and is visible from nearly every corner of the fort.

But what is fascinating about the fort is its security system. With multiple layers of defence and a number of booby traps, Daulatabad Fort boasts one of the most advanced security mechanisms of its time.

For starters, the fort had only one entrance and one exit. It was like a one-way trip inside — getting in was deceptively easy, but coming out? That was at your own risk.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!_OHc!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6fb9809d-00d6-402c-979d-1341ec525bd0_250x188.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!_OHc!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6fb9809d-00d6-402c-979d-1341ec525bd0_250x188.jpeg)

As the armies approached Daulatabad Fort, they were met not with a grand welcome, but with a zigzagging entrance, a tactical marvel designed to slow down their advance and sow confusion.

There were no parallel gates to charge through. Instead, the real gates were placed to the right, while false gates on the left lured invaders into dead ends — perfect spots for ambushes.

This wasn’t just a path — it was a labyrinth of deception, where every turn tested the enemy’s resolve and coordination. The narrow passages forced soldiers to break formation, making them vulnerable to attacks from above and the sides.

The main door of Daulatabad Fort is not just imposing — it’s armored with massive iron spikes, a tactical feature designed to deter elephant charges.

Back in the days before artillery and gunpowder, elephants were the battering rams of war. Trained to smash through gates and walls, they were a terrifying force on the battlefield.

But Daulatabad’s architects were a step ahead. The spikes on the gate were placed at precisely the height of an elephant’s forehead, ensuring that any attempt to ram the door would result in severe injury or death to the animal — effectively neutralizing one of the most powerful siege weapons of the time.

This wasn’t just cruelty — it was strategic deterrence, a message to invaders: brute force alone wouldn’t win this battle.

Once the invading armies broke through the spiked main door, they weren’t met with triumph — they were met with a steep, cobblestone ascent, designed to trip horses and scatter formations.

The path was narrow and unforgiving. Cobblestones made footing unstable, especially for cavalry, while the incline forced attackers to slow down and struggle uphill.

But the real terror came from above. Hidden cubby holes and murder slots lined the walls, where defending soldiers waited with boiling water, hot oil, and even molten metal. As the invaders stumbled forward, they were scalded from above — caught in a deadly funnel of heat and confusion.

This wasn’t just defense — it was psychological warfare, turning the fort itself into a weapon.

As the invading armies pushed deeper into Daulatabad Fort, they were met with yet another obstacle: a narrow drawbridge suspended over a deep, wide moat. Once drawn back, the bridge left attackers stranded — helpless and exposed.

This moat wasn’t just a trench — it was once filled with crocodiles, adding a layer of primal terror to the siege. Even if soldiers managed to cross the bridge, they faced the wrath of the Mendha Tope — a massive cannon mounted on a bastion, rotating 360 degrees, firing relentlessly at the advancing troops.

The cannon’s rear was shaped like a ram’s head, and it bore inscriptions from the Holy Quran, along with the names of Emperor Aurangzeb and the artisan Md. Arab who crafted it. It was both a weapon and a symbol of divine protection.

Only after surviving this brutal gauntlet did the soldiers reach the courtyard of the main building, where the final confrontation awaited

This is where the most formidable obstacle awaited the invaders.

After crossing the moat and surviving cannon fire, the soldiers found themselves at the entrance of a pitch-dark tunnel — a sinister passage known as the Bhul Bhulaiya, or maze.

This wasn’t just a tunnel — it was a network of false passages, secret chambers, and dead ends, carved into the volcanic rock. Once inside, the light vanished. The walls closed in. And the sense of direction dissolved.

Now imagine being an invading soldier. As they stumbled through the maze, the defenders sealed off all escape routes and filled the tunnel with smoke, disorienting and suffocating the intruders. Panic set in. The fort didn’t just repel enemies — it swallowed them whole.

As the soldiers grope for breath inside the smoke-filled Bhul Bhulaiya, a faint glimmer of light appears. Desperate and disoriented, they rush toward it, believing it to be salvation.

But it’s no exit — it’s a ramp leading straight into the moat. Wide, deep, and once filled with crocodiles, the moat becomes a watery grave. There’s no way out. The fort has turned hope into horror.

And for those few who do manage to find a real exit route? They’re greeted not with freedom, but with a guillotine, placed precisely at the tunnel’s mouth. As soon as a soldier steps out — off with his head. The trap is complete. The fort doesn’t just defend — it executes.

Daulatabad Fort is truly one of the greatest architectural marvels and defensive masterpieces in India. Its layered security system, ingenious traps, and psychological warfare tactics make it a fortress unlike any other.

Located just 17 km from Aurangabad, Daulatabad Fort (also known as Devagiri Fort) stands atop a 200-meter conical hill, offering panoramic views and a journey through centuries of history. From its spiked gates and crocodile-filled moat to the pitch-dark Bhul Bhulaiya maze and 360-degree rotating cannon, every inch of the fort was designed to repel, confuse, and defeat invaders.
